q: What is the primary function of a camera as an optical instrument?
a: forms and records an image of an object

q: How may the image in a camera be recorded?
a: on film (or) it may be detected by an electronic sensor

q: What type of image does a convex lens in a camera form on the film or electronic sensor?
What is the purpose of the diaphragm in a camera?
a: to control the amount of light energy falling on the film
to adjust the focal length of the lens

q: Where is the diaphragm placed in a simple camera?
a: in front of the convex lens

q: What is the aperture in a camera?
a: a circular opening through which the light passes
the main lens of the camera

q: How is the aperture in a camera made wide or narrow?
In what terms is the size or diameter of the aperture expressed?
What is the f-number of a camera defined as?
a: the ratio of the focal length of the lens to the diameter of aperture
the ratio of exposure time to shutter speed
the magnification power of the lens

q: What does a smaller f-number indicate about the aperture?
a: the shorter the focal length is

q: When taking a photograph in bright light, what type of f-number and aperture should be used?
What mechanism controls the time during which light enters the camera through the lens?
How is the shutter speed expressed in a camera?
a: in terms of the exposure time

q: What relationship exists between a shorter exposure time and shutter speed?
a: the higher the shutter speed is
the lower the shutter speed is
the exposure time is unrelated to shutter speed

q: What is the function of the focusing ring in a camera?
a: to adjust the distance between the lens and the film to obtain a sharp image
to control the amount of light

q: What is the film at the back of the camera described as?
a: a screen which receives the image

q: What does the film consist of?
a: a thin layer of chemical compounds of silver

q: What process occurs when light energy falls on the film?
In a digital camera, what replaces the film?
What is an electronic sensor in a digital camera made up of?
a: millions of tiny pixels which response to light

q: What is the primary function of the human eye as a sense organ?
a: receiving visual images and sending the information to the brain

q: Which part of the human eye is similar to the lens cover of a camera?
What is the front portion of the eye covered by?
a: a tough, transparent membrane called the cornea

q: What transparent liquid fills the region between the cornea and the eyelens?
What transparent liquid is found in the region between the eyelens and the retina?
Which part of the eye is the colored portion and controls the amount of light energy entering the eye?
Where is the pupil located?
a: in the centre of the iris

q: Which part of the eye is similar to the aperture in the camera?
How does the iris adjust the size of the pupil?
a: The iris adjusts the size of the pupil in the eye
The ciliary muscles adjust the size of the pupil
The retina controls the pupil size

q: How does the pupil behave in dim light?
What is the function of the ciliary muscles?
a: to vary the thickness of the eyelens, thus alter the focal length of the lens
to control the amount of light entering the eye
to transmit light energy to the brain

q: When viewing near objects, what happens to the ciliary muscles and the shape of the eyelens?
a: the ciliary muscles contract and permit the eyelens to form a more convex shape
the ciliary muscles relax and the eyelens becomes less convex
the ciliary muscles contract and the eyelens becomes less convex

q: What is the retina?
a: the light sensitive area of the cells, at the back of the eye
the tough transparent membrane at the front of the eye
the colored portion of the eye

q: What two types of cells does the retina consist of?
Which spot on the retina is the most light-sensitive and used for viewing very small objects?
What type of cells exclusively constitutes the yellow spot?
a: a mix of rods and cones

q: What is the blind spot on the retina?
a: a spot on the retina which has no rods or cones
the point where the ciliary muscles attach

q: What transmits the light energy falling on the retina to the brain?
Which of the following are considered refracting substances in the eye?
a: The cornea, the aqueous humour, the eyelens and the vitreous humour
The iris, the pupil, the retina

q: What kind of image is formed on the retina by the refracting substances of the eye?
a: real, inverted and smaller than the size of the object
virtual, upright and larger than the size of the object
real, upright and larger than the size of the object

q: What are the two common defects of vision mentioned?
In farsightedness (hyperopia), how are distant and nearby objects seen?
a: distant objects are seen clearly, but objects nearby are blurry
near objects are seen clearly, but objects far away are blurry

q: What are the causes of farsightedness?
a: an eyeball which is too short or due to the eyelens which cannot be sufficiently convex
an eyeball which is too long or an eyelens which is too convex
ciliary muscles that contract too much

q: How can farsightedness be corrected?
a: by the use of spectacles with converging lens or convex lens
by the use of spectacles with diverging lens or concave lens
by surgery to lengthen the eyeball

q: In nearsightedness (myopia), how are near and far objects seen?
a: near objects are seen clearly, but objects far away are blurry
distant objects are seen clearly, but objects nearby are blurry

q: What are the causes of nearsightedness?
a: an eyeball which is too long or due to the eyelens which is more convex than is necessary
an eyeball which is too short or an eyelens which is not sufficiently convex
ciliary muscles that relax too much

q: How can nearsightedness be corrected?
a: by the use of spectacles with diverging lens or concave lens
by the use of spectacles with converging lens or convex lens
by surgery to shorten the eyeball

q: For normal vision, what is the least distance of distinct vision?
For normal vision, what is the maximum distance of distinct vision?
When objects at infinity are viewed by people with normal vision, how are the eyes described?
What is the primary purpose of a microscope?
a: to make enlarged images of objects that are too small for the unaided eye to see
to project distant objects onto a screen

q: How many convex lenses does a compound microscope have at minimum?
a: a concave and a convex lens

q: What are the two main lenses in a compound microscope called?
a: an objective lens and an eyepiece lens
a condenser lens and a field lens
a primary lens and a secondary lens

q: Which lens in a compound microscope is nearer the object?
What kind of focal length does the objective lens of a compound microscope typically have?
Where should the object be placed when using a compound microscope?
a: between $F_o$ and $2F_o$, where $F_o$ is the focus of the objective
at the focus of the objective

q: What are the characteristics of the image produced by the objective lens in a compound microscope?
a: real, inverted and larger than the size of the object
virtual, upright and smaller than the size of the object
real, upright and larger than the size of the object

q: Where is the image formed by the objective lens located relative to the eyepiece?
a: between the eyepiece E and its focus $F_e$
at the focus of the eyepiece

q: What are the characteristics of the final image formed by the eyepiece in a compound microscope?
a: virtual and on the same side as the object
real and on the opposite side as the object

q: Under what condition can the final image from a compound microscope be seen clearly?
a: when the eye is very close to the eyepiece
when the eye is far from the eyepiece
when the objective lens is adjusted

q: What is the magnifying power (MP) of a compound microscope defined as?
a: the product of the magnification of the objective and the magnifying power of the eyepiece
the sum of the magnification of the objective and the eyepiece
the ratio of the magnification of the objective to the eyepiece

q: What is the formula for the separation of the lenses in a compound microscope?
When the final image in a compound microscope is formed at infinity for normal setting, what is the relationship for the eyepiece object distance?
What focal length characteristics are required for a large magnifying power of a compound microscope?
a: both the objective and the eyepiece should have short focal lengths
both the objective and the eyepiece should have long focal lengths
the objective should have a long focal length and the eyepiece a short focal length

q: For a large magnifying power, how does the focal length of the objective compare to that of the eyepiece?
a: the focal length of the objective should be shorter than that of the eyepiece
the focal length of the objective should be longer than that of the eyepiece
the focal lengths should be equal

q: What happens to the sharpness of images as the magnifying power of compound microscopes increases?
a: the sharpness of images is reduced
the sharpness of images is increased
the sharpness of images remains constant

q: What are compound microscopes commonly used for?
What is an electron microscope characterized by?
What does an electron microscope employ instead of light?
What type of lenses do electron microscopes use?
What is the maximum magnifying power of optical microscopes?
What is the magnifying power that electron microscopes can reach?
Why do electron microscopes achieve greater magnification than optical microscopes?
What is used as a source of illumination in an electron microscope?
What are the two main types of electron microscopes mentioned in the text?
a: the Scanning Electron Microscope (SEM) and the Transmission Electron Microscope (TEM)
the Reflecting Electron Microscope (REM) and the Refracting Electron Microscope (REM)
the Digital Electron Microscope (DEM) and the Analog Electron Microscope (AEM)

q: How is the accelerated electron beam in an electron microscope focused?
What information does the electron beam carry when it emerges from the specimen?
a: information about the structure of the specimen
information about the chemical composition of the specimen
information about the temperature of the specimen

q: How can the image of a specimen in an electron microscope be viewed?
a: by projecting the magnified electron image onto a fluorescent viewing screen
by observing it directly with the naked eye
by reflecting it onto a mirror

q: Electron microscopes are used to investigate the ultrastructure of what types of specimens?
a: a wide range of biological and inorganic specimens, metals, and crystals

q: What is an optical instrument used for viewing distant objects called?
What are the two types of telescopes mentioned in the text?
What type of lenses does a refracting astronomical telescope consist of?
a: an objective of long focal length and an eyepiece of short focal length
an objective of short focal length and an eyepiece of long focal length

q: Who invented a refracting telescope?
What type of lens is used for both the objective and the eyepiece in a refracting astronomical telescope?
What are the characteristics of the image formed by the objective of an astronomical telescope for a distant object?
a: a real and inverted image formed at the focus of the objective
a virtual and upright image formed at the focus of the objective
a real and upright image formed beyond the focus

q: Where is the image formed by the objective lens located relative to the eyepiece in an astronomical telescope?
a: within the focal length of the eyepiece
beyond the focal length of the eyepiece
at the focal point of the eyepiece

q: What are the characteristics of the final image formed by the eyepiece in an astronomical telescope?
a: virtual, larger than the first image and inverted with respect to the distant object
real, smaller than the first image and upright with respect to the distant object
virtual, smaller than the first image and inverted

q: How is the magnifying power (MP) of a refracting astronomical telescope defined?
a: the ratio of the angle $\beta$ subtended by the image seen by the eye through the optical instrument to the angle $\alpha$ subtended by the object as seen without instrument
the sum of the focal lengths of the objective and eyepiece
the product of the magnification of the objective and eyepiece

q: When the final image in an astronomical telescope is formed at infinity, what is the magnifying power (MP) formula?
What is the formula for the separation of the lenses in an astronomical telescope when the final image is formed at infinity?
To achieve a high magnifying power in a refracting astronomical telescope, what are the requirements for the objective and eyepiece focal lengths?
a: an objective of long focal length and an eyepiece of short focal length are required
an objective of short focal length and an eyepiece of long focal length are required
both objective and eyepiece should have long focal lengths

q: To obtain sharp images of distant objects, what characteristic should the objective lens of an astronomical telescope have?
Why is a concave mirror sometimes used as an objective in astronomical telescopes instead of a large diameter convex lens?
a: it is difficult to make a perfect convex lens of large diameter free from defects

q: What are most telescopes used at astronomical observatories in the world called?
Who first suggested the reflecting telescope design?
What is the Newtonian design also known as?
In a reflecting telescope, what happens to parallel rays from a distant object after striking the concave mirror?
a: they are reflected from the concave mirror and then allowed to fall on a plane mirror
they are refracted through the concave mirror

q: Why is an astronomical telescope not convenient for viewing objects on Earth?
a: the final image produced is inverted
the final image produced is too small
the final image produced is blurry

q: How is a terrestrial telescope modified from an astronomical telescope?
a: to produce a final image which is erect
to produce a larger final image
to produce a brighter final image

q: What type of lens is used as an eyepiece in a terrestrial telescope to produce an erect final image?
Why are total reflecting prisms used in binoculars?
a: to reduce the length of the terrestrial telescope

q: What is a binoculars?
a: an optical instrument which consists of a pair of telescopes one for each eye

q: How do the prisms in binoculars reduce the telescope's length?
a: Because of the successive total internal reflections in the prisms

q: What branch of physics studies the nature and properties of light?
What does the acronym LASER stand for?
a: Light Amplification by Stimulated Emission of Radiation
Light Application for Space Energy Research
Luminous Amplitude of Synchronized Emitted Rays

q: What are the characteristics of the light produced by a laser?
a: narrow beam of intense, monochromatic, coherent light
wide beam of diffuse, polychromatic, incoherent light
narrow beam of faint, polychromatic, incoherent light

q: What part of the electromagnetic radiation spectrum can laser light be in?
What process does a laser produce?
a: stimulated emission of photons from excited atoms or molecules

q: What is the typical power range for commercial lasers in a continuous beam?
a: a fraction of a milliwatt to around 20 kilowatts

q: What are the five main types of lasers classified in the text?
a: gas lasers, solid-state lasers, fiber lasers, liquid lasers (dye lasers), semiconductor lasers (laser diodes)
chemical lasers, excimer lasers, free-electron lasers, X-ray lasers
argon lasers, ruby lasers, CO2 lasers, fiber-optic lasers, diode lasers

q: What are the three essential elements in a laser?
a: laser medium, power supply (for pumping process) and optical cavity
focusing lens, cooling system, external detector
aiming mechanism, energy storage, beam splitter

q: What is the laser medium?
a: the material emitting the laser beam

q: What materials can serve as a laser medium?
a: a gas, liquid, crystalline solid or semiconductor crystal

q: What is the function of the power supply in a laser?
a: delivers energy to the laser medium in the form needed to excite it to emit light

q: What is the process called when energy is supplied to the laser medium to excite it?
What is the purpose of the optical cavity (or resonator) in a laser?
a: concentrates the light to stimulate the emission of laser radiation

q: What does the optical cavity consist of?
What is the reflectivity of the mirror that completely reflects light in the optical cavity?
What is the reflectivity of the mirror that allows some light to come out as a laser beam?
What are the two essential processes to initiate a laser action?
In stimulated emission, what happens when an excited atom interacts with an incident photon?
a: radiates photons of the same frequency in the same direction and in phase with the stimulating radiation
moves to a higher energy state

q: What condition is needed for stimulated emission to occur regarding population levels?
a: the population of the upper level of a transition would have to be higher than that of the lower level
the population of the lower level would be higher than the upper level

q: What is the result when a photon is more likely to stimulate emission than to be absorbed?
To sustain laser action, what must happen to the lower and upper energy levels?
a: the lower level must be depopulated while the upper level is populated
both levels must be populated equally

q: Which characteristic describes laser emission as coherent?
a: the light waves it contains are in phase with one another
the light waves have different frequencies
the light waves are randomly oriented

q: What is a typical beam divergence for a laser beam mentioned in the text?
What is an example given to illustrate high intensity of a modest laser power?
a: 1 mW He-Ne laser beam is much brighter than the sunlight
1 W incandescent bulb is brighter than a laser
a flashlight beam is equivalent to a laser beam

q: What application of lasers is made possible by its coherence property?
Which of the following is an application of lasers in medicine?
In communication, how is laser light used in optical fibre communications?
a: to send information over large distances

q: What is the purpose of laser range finders?
a: to determine the distance to an object

q: In industries, what are high power lasers used for?
a: to cut metals, drill aerosol nozzles and orifices

q: How is laser light used in shops and business establishments?

q: Lasers are used in semiconductor industries for what purpose?

to encrypt data on hard drives

q: On what principle does fibre optics work?
How does fibre optics transmit information?
a: as light pulses along a glass or plastic fibre
as electrical signals through copper wires

q: How many glass fibres can a fibre optic cable contain?
a: from a few up to hundreds

q: What are the two concentric layers of an optical fibre called?
What surrounds the glass fibre core in an optical fibre?
What surrounds the cladding in an optical fibre?
What is the purpose of the sheath (plastic buffer) in an optical fibre?
How do the refractive indices of the core and cladding compare?
a: The refractive index of core will be slightly greater than that of cladding
The refractive index of cladding will be slightly greater than that of core

q: Under what condition does total internal reflection occur in an optical fibre?
a: if the light ray is injected and strikes the core to cladding interface at an angle greater than the critical angle
if the light ray strikes at an angle less than the critical angle
if the light ray is absorbed by the cladding

q: What happens if the angle of incidence at the core to cladding interface is less than the critical angle?
a: both reflection and refraction take place

q: What causes the light beam to attenuate and die off over a certain distance if the angle of incidence is less than the critical angle?
What is a useful application of fibre optics in medicine?
What is endoscopy defined as?
a: a non-surgical procedure to examine the interior of a hollow organ or cavity of the human body
a surgical procedure to remove tumors
a diagnostic test using external radiation

q: How do endoscopes use optical fibres?
a: to observe an image inside the body

q: What is inserted into the body to monitor interior organs clearly using fibre optics?
a: a camera with a conventional lens

q: What is a spectrometer?
a: a scientific instrument used to separate and measure spectral components of a physical phenomenon

q: In which disciplines is a spectrometer a widely used scientific tool?
What are the components of an optical spectrometer?
a: light source, collimator, dispersion elements and a telescope
a camera, a mirror, a focusing ring
a film, a sensor, a shutter

q: What disperses the collimated beam from a specific light source in an optical spectrometer?
a: dispersion element such as prism or grating

q: What information can be determined by rotating the telescope and measuring the angle of dispersion for each spectral line?
a: the wavelength; and hence, the chemical make up of the source
the intensity of the light source

q: In a spectrophotometer, what does the dispersion element do?
a: separates light into spectrum which is allowed to fall on the sample to be investigated
focuses the light onto a screen

q: What allows for the identification of materials in a spectrophotometer?
a: The change in the absorption and emission of the light intensity with wavelength
The change in the temperature of the sample
The change in the color of the sample

q: What are three of the most common optical spectrometers mentioned?
a: spectrophotometers (UV-Vis spectrometer), spectrofluorometers and Raman spectrometers
X-ray spectrometers, mass spectrometers, nuclear magnetic resonance spectrometers
infrared spectrometers, atomic absorption spectrometers, emission spectrometers

q: What is photometry?
a: the science of measuring visible light in units that are weighted according to the sensitivity of human eye
the study of light interaction with matter
the measurement of all electromagnetic radiation

q: What is the wavelength range of light that the human eye responds to?
To what color of light is the human eye most sensitive?
What are the four main photometric quantities?
a: luminous flux, luminous intensity, luminance, and illuminance

q: What does luminous flux (luminous power) $\Phi$ measure?
a: the power of visible light produced by a light source
the intensity of light in a specific direction
the amount of light incident on a surface

q: What is the SI unit for luminous flux?
How much power does one lumen (1 lm) of light with a wavelength of 555 nm represent?
What does luminous intensity $I_V$ measure?
a: the luminous flux emitted by a light source in a direction per unit solid angle
the total luminous flux produced by a source
the amount of light falling on a surface

q: What is the formula for luminous intensity $I_V$?
What is the SI basic unit for luminous intensity?
What is a solid angle $\Omega$ a measure of?
a: the amount of the field of view from some particular point that a given object covers
the length of a curved line
the area of a flat surface

q: What is the formula for a solid angle $\Omega$?
What is the SI system unit for a solid angle?
What is luminance $L_V$?
a: the luminous intensity per unit area of light travelling in a given direction
the total luminous flux incident on a surface
the power of visible light produced by a source

q: What is the SI unit for luminance?
What does illuminance $E_V$ measure?
a: the total luminous flux incident on a surface per unit area
the luminous intensity per unit area

q: Which of the following formulas represents illuminance $E_V$ in terms of luminous flux $\Phi$, luminous intensity $I_V$, solid angle $\Omega$, and illuminated surface area $A$?

What is the formula for illuminance $E_V$ on a surface at a distance $r$ from a source of luminous intensity $I_V$ where $\theta$ is the angle between the direction of light and normal to the illuminated surface?
What is the SI unit for illuminance?
What is the minimum average illuminance recommended for reading areas in school study halls?
According to the Inverse Square Law of Illuminance, how is illuminance $E_V$ at any point on a surface related to the distance $r$ from the source?
a: inversely proportional to the square of the distance $r$

q: In the formula $E_V = \frac{I_V \cos \theta}{r^2}$, what does $\theta$ represent?
a: angle between the direction of light and normal to the illuminated surface
